- Levels of Wealth: Inside The Secret Lives of The Ultra-Rich: Kind: captions Language: en-GB If you earned $1 per second. Then to make 1 million dollars would take you about 11 and a half days. But to make 1 billion dollars, would take you over 31 and a half years. You see, it’s easy to think of wealth in terms of rich or poor, but broadly speaking, there are arguably 5 different levels of rich, and at each level, things change very drastically. The difference between what a millionaire can do, and a billionaire can do, is simply staggering. In this video we’ll be exploring what life is like at each of these different wealth levels, and delving into how the ultra-rich spend their money, including some of their most bizarre and extravagant purchases. But, as you’re watching, you’ll realise that one of the biggest changes that happens at each wealth level, is perspective. Because prices feel relative to how much you earn. So if you compare someone whose earning $40,000 a year with someone earning 400 million a year, it’s 10,000 times more. Which means: A lambo that costs $300,000, to them is the equivalent of spending $30. A $10 million piece of art? Feels like $1,000. A $50 million home in the Hamptons? Feels like $5,000. So whilst to us those purchases would feel so major, to a billionaire, it’s not even something they need to think about. And of course, when you’re that wealthy, it’s not just items and experiences you can buy. But we’ll get to that. Let’s instead start off, with the first level of rich: The Top 1% Even though this phrase is synonymous with being extremely rich, if your income is over about $200,000 a year, you would technically be part of the top 1% of worldwide earners. And so being in the top 1% may not make you feel quite as rich as you think. Because sure, you can typically afford a nice house and car, but with those things comes higher costs, and so you probably don’t feel very financially secure, and you’re likely still working a full time job. Which is why beyond this level, wealth starts being measured in net worth rather than income, as the more wealth you have, the more assets you have, and you’re probably not making your money from a salary. So the next level of rich is a Net Worth of $10 Million - $30 Million At this level, your needs are met. You can live very comfortably. You have a really nice house. You often fly first class internationally. If it’s a special occasion, you might book a $2,000 suite. You can afford any healthcare you need, and in fact no emergency financial situation can destroy your life. But you are still not "rich" in the way that money doesn't matter. You still have to be sensible and careful with big decisions. And in the banking world, you’re still not classified as ultra high net worth'. Which brings us to out next level: Net Worth of $30 Million - $100 Million At this point, you start playing in the big leagues. And most likely you run or have a controlling interest in a very large company. You always stay at 5 star hotels, and you have multiple residences all around the world you can stay at as well. You take holidays during prime time, for example going to Monaco for the Grand Prix, or Cannes for the film festival. And you won’t bat an eyelid when the place you’re staying at costs $10-$20,000 per NIGHT. You have personal assistants, and are starting to have ‘people’ that others have to talk to to get to you. You can buy pretty much anything that normal people think of as ‘stuff rich people buy. Like you can get any cars you want, the fanciest jewellery, and so on. Instead of flying first class, you fly private. Although you’d typically charter a flight or own a private jet fractionally through a service like Net Jets, instead of buying it outright and having to maintain it. But at this level, your social circle changes dramatically too. You probably socialize with Congressmen, Senators and community leaders. You are likely very well respected within some very high class circles. Interestingly though, since you’re likely surrounded by other ultra wealthy people, you may still feel you don’t have enough. Sure, you may have just bought a new Ferrari, but your friend has a limited edition version where only five of them were ever made. Sure, you just had dinner with a local governor, but your billionaire friend just had dinner with the President. Which brings us to our next level of wealth: Net Worth of $100 Million - $1 Billion To be at this level, you likely have ownership or control of a business that most of the public have heard of, even if they haven’t heard of you directly. At this point, you can likely socialize with movie stars, rock stars, and the corporate elite. You can go pretty much anywhere you want, and always in style. You have multiple residences with staff, all over the world, with elite cars at every residence. If it’s your thing, you might have a private island. And of course a private yacht to get you there. Almost any experience you can dream up, you can have. Want to play tennis with your favourite tennis star? Call his people. For a donation of 100k+ to his charity, you can probably play a match with him. Want your favourite band to come and play at your private party? For the right price, they will. Love art? Your people could arrange for the curator of the Louvre to show you around and show you some masterpieces they haven’t exhibited in years. If you can dream an experience up, you can likely have it - donations and fees can get you to anyone. Once you have many hundreds of millions of dollars, it’s the same when buying things - you may hire people to find you the most extravagant versions of items possible. For example, the Australian company Toilet Paper Man produces 22-carat gold toilet paper, for a cost of over 1.3 million. And if you are going to quite literally flush money down the toilet, why not get a gold toilet or entire gold bathroom to go with it? In fact, if you’re ultra-wealthy, you can literally have gold everything. How about a 24 karat gold water bottle, which in itself is a piece of designer art, and comes with spring water from France and Fiji, combined with glacier water from iceland. Or alternatively, how about the world’s most expensive iPhone has 24 carat gold casing, and more than 600 white and black diamonds, and sapphire glass for the screen. This certainly isn’t available to the public, and to buy one you’d need over 15 million dollars. At this level, it’s not just about buying the fanciest most luxurious items, it’s about getting the rarest and most exclusive things you possibly can. You could get a limited edition pen, that costs $160,000, has 4,810 diamonds inside, and only 8 are made each year. Or if you want to go a lot bigger than that, many billionaires turn to art. Not just rare paintings, but things like this dead shark art piece created by Damien Hurst that sold for $12 million dollars. Or let’s say you like piano? Well, here’s the same crystal piano that was used for the Beijing Olympic Games. It was purchased at auction by a private bidder for a record $3.22 million U.S. dollars! Your mind is the only real limit to what is available. But Our final level, $1 Billion Net Worth and Above. Before we get into what you can do as a billionaire - which is pretty insane - it’s worth noting that the number of billionaires on Forbes' annual rich list has significantly increased in recent years, and there’s now around 2,755 known billionaires. Of course, it’s possible there are more who have generational wealth that simply do a good job of staying very very private. Either way, Forbes estimated these 2,755 billionaires are worth a combined $13.1 trillion, and therefore have more wealth between them than the poorest 5 billion people on earth combined. In fact, Oxfam have argued that the very richest 26 billionaires have more wealth between them than the poorest 50% of the entire world. These statistics are overwhelming, but let’s actually visualise it to put things in perspective. This rice example really highlights that even within this billionaire category, the difference between 1 billion and the kind of wealth Jeff Bezos or Elon Musk has, is insanely vast. I’m a big fan of James Jani’s YouTube channel, and he also did a great job of visualising the difference between these large sums of money in a different way: So, now that we’ve got a better idea of the scale of a billion dollars, let’s look at what you can do with it. Because essentially, you can buy anything. And I do mean, anything. We’re no longer just talking about luxurious items and experiences. Firstly, you can buy access. You can ask your staff to contact almost anyone on the planet and get a call back. It’s amazing the level of access and respect a billion can get you, where within an hour you could ask your people to get in touch with someone, and they’d be able to do it. It’s not just celebrities either, it works the same with powerful people within politics too - especially because the billionaire may well be a donor to their political party. And so, if you’re a billionaire you may occasionally meet with heads-of-states and have conversations with them. Which brings us to the second thing a billion dollars can buy: Influence. As a billionaire, you have many ways to shape public policy and the public debate. This isn’t always done in some kind of evil way, like lobbying for lower taxes for rich people (although that obviously does happen). But because of your wealth and connections, you can absolutely influence big changes in the world, and not just on a political level. Because Another thing you can buy is IMPACT, through philanthropy and charity. Your money can literally change countless lives. The question is, how do you distribute it? Clean water for villages? Fund new hospitals? Try to save a dying species of animal? With money, you can impact almost anything of your choice. But one of the biggest differences with billionaires that is often overlooked, is the ability to essentially buy time. You quite literally don’t have wait for anything. No lines. No sitting around. Just think about it: You’ve got your own private jet, so you just show up at the airport whenever you want, sit down in the plane and 2 minutes later you’re in the sky and can go where you wish. When you arrive, a driver will have been arranged to pick you up and take you wherever you want to go. Your assistant will have already got you the best table at the restaurant you mentioned wanting to eat at. Or maybe your assistant arranged for a celebrity chef to come to one of your vacation homes instead, at whatever time you choose. However… believe it or not, there are some downsides to being this ultra wealthy. Firstly, it’s nearly impossible to have normal emotional relationships at this level. It can become increasingly difficult to have friends and family that love you for who you are. They exist, but it is pretty hard to know which ones they are. Because, your world is likely filled with yes men, and people looking to take advantage at every opportunity. Your trust and connections with people are undoubtedly considerably weakened. Dating also becomes an odd experience. On the one hand, some of the world’s most beautiful and intelligent women are all around you at parties and events, but again, finding someone you have a genuine emotional connection with is a different story… especially because you’re time is so valuable that you’re always busy and unable to invest in making deep connections with new people. And even if you do find someone, the relationship balanced is skewed in a strange way when you have essentially unlimited money - no matter whether you pay for everything or whether your partner makes their own money, either way something feels off. And finally, and most crucially, no level of money can give you everything. Death is the great equalizer, and losing loved ones, and losing your own health and facing your own mortality, are problems that no amount of money can solve. The reddit poster who inspired this video and deserves full credit, talks about how he met Sylvester Stallone at a party, who has obviously had a very successful career. He said he was such a great guy, had huge levels of wealth and luxury, a smart and beautiful wife. And yet, he also had a special needs son who died young. In his words, Nobody has it all. Nobody. In fact, if you were watching this video and feeling poor, I think it’s worth re-thinking. Because in this video we’ve looked at several levels of wealth, but the fact you are watching this means you are nowhere close to bottom of the total wealth pyramid. It’s easy to look up and feel frustrated that some people have so much more than us, but just look down the wealth pyramid for a moment. If you have internet access to watch this, plus some food in the fridge, clothes on your back, a roof over your head and a place to sleep… … then you are richer than 75 per cent of this world. In fact, even if you have $1 to your name right now, you are still richer than the majority of the world who are either in debt, or have absolutely nothing. What this video really highlights is that rich vs poor is so arbitrary. It’s not two groups, there are many many levels. And compared to most of the world, you might well be a lot richer than you think… If you enjoyed this video, please make sure you subscribe and turn on notifications for some future mini-documentaries that are on the way. Thank you for watching to the end, you are a legend, and I’ll see you next time. Cheers!

Living in a house with more bathrooms and bedrooms, designer furniture, the latest technology programmed to make everything comfortable. In the past, people found meaning through the things that they were creating. The job wasn't just for a job. It wasn't just a paycheck. It was something that you contributed back to community and people took pride in their work. That's why you see old staircases that are so beautiful, old buildings that look like masterpieces. And now we don't value the same things anymore. First, a lot of the jobs out there are just jobs programs. They're just invented for us to be doing something. Really, no wonder that we feel so detached with the things that we do. And so we have to find meaning elsewhere. And that's where consumerism comes in. We find meaning in the things that we buy and what those things represent about us. But no amount of stuff or how much we accessorize our lives with cool glittery stuff, it will not give us the same meaning and purpose as the things that we create. With all that said, you do not need to quit your job and trade it in for a creative job. We can all create in other ways. Try to cook a meal from scratch. Plant something and watch it grow. Learn to play an instrument. Try to do a DIY house project on your own. Do it with love and pride. And not only will you feel more connected to yourself, you're proving that your value and worth comes from within. I think it's safe to say that we all want a wealthy life. But I hope that you don't get distracted by this performance of what wealth is by the mainstream and social media. I hope that this video gave you something to think about how you should define your own version of wealth. And honestly, I think that most of us don't realize how wealthy we already are. If you are watching this video, you're probably watching it on your phone or your laptop. If you have a warm bed to sleep in, if you have a roof over your head, if you have people or animals that love you, you're already so much more wealthier than you think. You just have to see it for yourself.

- Mansa Musa, one of the wealthiest people who ever lived - Jessica Smith: Kind: captions Language: en If someone asked you who the richest people in history were, who would you name? Perhaps a billionaire banker or corporate mogul, like Bill Gates or John D. Rockefeller. How about African King Musa Keita I? Ruling the Mali Empire in the 14th century CE, Mansa Musa, or the King of Kings, amassed a fortune that possibly made him one of the wealthiest people who ever lived. But his vast wealth was only one piece of his rich legacy. When Mansa Musa came to power in 1312, much of Europe was racked by famine and civil wars. But many African kingdoms and the Islamic world were flourishing, and Mansa Musa played a great role in bringing the fruits of this flourishing to his own realm. By strategically annexing the city of Timbuktu, and reestablishing power over the city of Gao, he gained control over important trade routes between the Mediterranean and the West African Coast, continuing a period of expansion, which dramatically increased Mali's size. The territory of the Mali Empire was rich in natural resources, such as gold and salt. The world first witnessed the extent of Mansa Musa's wealth in 1324 when he took his pilgrimage to Mecca. Not one to travel on a budget, he brought a caravan stretching as far as the eye could see. Accounts of this journey are mostly based on an oral testimony and differing written records, so it's difficult to determine the exact details. But what most agree on is the extravagant scale of the excursion. Chroniclers describe an entourage of tens of thousands of soldiers, civilians, and slaves, 500 heralds bearing gold staffs and dressed in fine silks, and many camels and horses bearing an abundance of gold bars. Stopping in cities such as Cairo, Mansa Musa is said to have spent massive quantities of gold, giving to the poor, buying souvenirs, and even having mosques built along the way. In fact, his spending may have destabilized the regional economy, causing mass inflation. This journey reportedly took over a year, and by the time Mansa Musa returned, tales of his amazing wealth had spread to the ports of the Mediterranean. Mali and its king were elevated to near legendary status, cemented by their inclusion on the 1375 Catalan Atlas. One of the most important world maps of Medieval Europe, it depicted the King holding a scepter and a gleaming gold nugget. Mansa Musa had literally put his empire and himself on the map. But material riches weren't the king's only concern. As a devout Muslim, he took a particular interest in Timbuktu, already a center of religion and learning prior to its annexation. Upon returning from his pilgrimage, he had the great Djinguereber Mosque built there with the help of an Andalusian architect. He also established a major university, further elevating the city's reputation, and attracting scholars and students from all over the Islamic world. Under Mansa Musa, the Empire became urbanized, with schools and mosques in hundreds of densely populated towns. The king's rich legacy persisted for generations and to this day, there are mausoleums, libraries and mosques that stand as a testament to this golden age of Mali's history.

- 7 Signs Someone is Secretly Wealthy: Kind: captions Language: es-MX La mayoría de los millonarios y multimillonarios son completamente invisibles. Probablemente estén sentados junto a ti en Starbucks, y ni siquiera sabes que tienen siete, ocho o quizá hasta nueve cifras en el banco. En mi opinión, hay siete señales reveladoras que delatan si alguien es rico en secreto o no, así que si sabes qué buscar, podrás encontrarlos. Sinceramente creo que si logras adoptar algunos de sus hábitos, podría ser la clave para construir una verdadera riqueza por tu cuenta. La primera señal que vamos a ver es que la gente rica evita a toda costa hablar de las tres E. Ahora, estas tres E se refieren a ganar, a lo caro y al exceso. Por la primera E, que es ganar, nunca hablan del dinero que están ganando porque para ellos, si alguien siempre está hablando de dinero y de cuánto gana, demuestra que esa persona todavía está en la etapa de querer probar algo. Los verdaderamente ricos no tienen nada que probar porque en secreto ya saben que tienen el dinero y no hay necesidad de decírselo a nadie. La segunda E: no presumen su última compra cara. Mientras otros publican bolsas de diseñador o relojes de lujo en redes, los ricos en secreto callan sus adquisiciones. Porque si alguien habla constantemente de cuánto costó su carro, de lo caras que fueron sus vacaciones, es una clara señal de que estiraron su presupuesto para poder pagarlo y están tratando de maximizar cada dólar que gastaron obteniendo algo de fama en redes sociales o quizá contándoselo a sus amigos. Y la tercera E es que evitan vivir en exceso, al menos en cuanto a su imagen externa. La gente rica entiende que los verdaderos lujos en la vida son tener opciones, comodidad y libertad, y no tener las cosas más ostentosas. Si alguna vez te encuentras con una persona que habla de cuánto gana, cuál fue la compra más cara que ha hecho recientemente, o simplemente vive en exceso y lo presume, puedes apostar que esa persona probablemente no es rica. ¿Te crees mucho por tu Range Rover? Pues sí. Al menos yo tengo carro. ¿Tú siquiera tienes uno? Quédate aquí. ¿Quién es el plebeyo ahora? ¡¿Quién es el plebeyo ahora?! Eso pensé. Okay. Entonces, eso significa lo contrario. Los que son callados, son ricos en secreto, y creo que si puedes adoptar este tipo de actitud en tu vida, podría ayudarte a generar más riqueza. Porque la energía hipotética que gastarías en las apariencias, o en impresionar a otros, podría redirigirse a construir tu patrimonio neto real. La señal número dos de que alguien es rico en secreto: conduce un auto confiable, de cinco a más de diez años, ya pagado. No manejan autos ostentosos como BMW de alta gama, Porsches, Rolls-Royces o Lamborghinis. Eso es porque los ricos saben lo brutal de la depreciación, así que tirar quinientos a ochocientos dólares al mes en la mensualidad de un carro por algo que pierde valor cada día es un retorno de inversión muy negativo. Y si haces cuentas, podrías invertir de seis mil a nueve mil seiscientos dólares anuales en su lugar, y en diez años con rendimientos compuestos, eso bien podría valer más de cien mil. Además, un auto de cinco a más de diez años ya perdió la mayor parte de su valor por depreciación. Las curvas de depreciación de autos varían, pero se aplanan drásticamente tras cinco años. Esto significa que la mayor parte de la depreciación ya ha ocurrido, por lo que el valor se estabiliza. Si quieres ahorrar dinero en cuanto a carros, creo que tiene mucho sentido comprar un carro usado con unas treinta mil millas o quizá de tres a cinco años de uso. Ese suele ser el punto ideal entre valor y depreciación, pero si solo quieres ser una persona rica que pasa desapercibida, quizá optarías por algo más viejo. Pueden ver en esta gráfica que estos son los modelos de los carros, y el "aquí" que se indica en la gráfica es el punto de inflexión alrededor del año seis o siete, y es cuando la depreciación realmente se desacelera. Los ricos en secreto entienden que un auto es solo transporte. Prefieren ese dinero extra en inversión, evitando pagos de auto y destinándolo a activos que generen riqueza. En otras palabras, prefieren ser ricos que solo parecer ricos y al mismo tiempo deber un montón de dinero por un carro. La señal número tres de que alguien es rico en secreto es que el dinero que sí gastan lo invierten en cosas que la gente normal ni siquiera consideraría. gastan su dinero en cosas poco comunes, por ejemplo, para tener más tiempo, pueden tener un pasatiempo de coleccionables raros, o gastan dinero en pequeños lujos o comodidades de las que no te enterarías a menos que, por alguna razón, pudieras pasar veinticuatro/siete con ellos todos los días. Hablemos primero del tiempo. Una persona rica entiende que el único recurso finito en todo el mundo y en la vida es el tiempo. Por lo tanto, podrían comprar su tiempo de vuelta y están dispuestos a gastar una cantidad desproporcionada de dinero en las cosas que pueden devolverles ese tiempo. En viajes, podrían volar en privado, evitando seguridad, saliendo cuando quieran y saltándose el tedioso abordaje y desembarque. Para la comida, un chef privado les hace las compras y cocina saludable, ahorrándoles incontables horas semanales. Y las tareas del hogar, también las delegan. Para eso, seguro tienen mucha ayuda. Y en todos estos casos de ahorro de tiempo, realmente no tendrías idea de que alguien gasta dinero en estos servicios a menos que pudieras observar a esta persona por un período prolongado. La siguiente categoría de gasto en la que una persona rica podría derrochar, es en pasatiempos poco comunes o caros como coleccionar arte raro, vinos finos, recuerdos deportivos o quizá hasta tarjetas de Pokémon. En lugar de mostrar su riqueza como una persona normal, prefieren hacerlo con una apreciación por el lujo y artículos raros. Poseer algo que cientos de miles ya tienen no es lo suficientemente genial para ellos. Necesitan sentirse únicos; cuanto más raro el artículo, más codiciado. En mi opinión, es naturaleza humana. Cubiertas las necesidades básicas, los ricos ya no compiten con otros. Están tratando de diferenciarse por completo de todos los demás, y una de las formas en que lo hacen es coleccionando bienes raros o quizá comprando su tiempo de vuelta, que es su activo más... eh, valioso. Otra parte de la naturaleza humana es suscribirse a mi boletín gratuito llamado Hump Days. Ahí publicamos noticias de negocios dos veces por semana, los miércoles y domingos. Y si quieres ser rico en secreto algún día y disfrutas los videos de aquí, asegúrate de suscribirte. Es completamente gratis en la descripción de abajo. Perdón, tenía que meter el comercial sutilmente. Bueno, continuemos. La señal número cuatro de que alguien es rico en secreto, es que son conscientes de su imagen. Ya aludí un poco a esto en la primera señal, pero la gente rica entiende que, por lo general, la gente siente envidia de lo que no tiene. Incluso si tu mejor amigo está feliz por ti el noventa y nueve por ciento de las veces que tienes éxito, siempre habrá un pequeño pensamiento en su mente, quizá en el fondo, de "¿Cómo sería si mi vida fuera como la de ellos?" o "¿Por qué no pude ser yo?". Esa es la naturaleza humana. Así que, para no incomodar a nadie, evitan hablar de cualquier cosa que pueda llevar a alguien a entender, por deducción, cuánto dinero podrían tener o cuán exitosos son. Si le preguntas a una persona rica cuál es su patrimonio neto, es probable que diga alguna frase como: "Me ha ido bastante bien", o "Ahí la llevo", o quizá, "Ciertamente no soy la persona más rica que conozco". Nick, no puedes pagarlo. ¿Tu familia es realmente rica? Cómodamente. Eso es exactamente lo que diría una persona súper rica. Y de hecho, esta es una historia real que me pasó. Estaba comiendo con un individuo que vale más de nueve cifras, o sea, al menos cien millones de dólares de patrimonio neto. Y cuando hablaba de sus amigos en la comida, todos multimillonarios, soltó algo en la conversación muy casualmente. Fue algo como: "Soy pobre en comparación con todos mis amigos". Para mí, fue una locura escuchar eso porque me demostró que a medida que subes en la escalera de la riqueza, siempre habrá alguien más rico con quien compararte. Digo, eso es cierto para casi todos en el mundo a menos que seas Elon Musk, claro pero sigue siendo una locura pensar que alguien en el Top 400 de Forbes, por ejemplo, todavía podría compararse con personas que están más arriba en la lista. La comparación está tan arraigada en la naturaleza humana. Estaría dispuesto a apostar que hay al menos una persona en la lista del Top 400 de Forbes que piensa que no es lo suficientemente rica, aunque racionalmente, están perfectamente bien. La gente rica quiere ser cuidadosa con su imagen externa también por razones de seguridad. Si son constantemente ostentosos, o le dicen a la gente cuál es su patrimonio neto, solo están atrayendo atención negativa y resentimiento de otros, lo que podría comprometer su seguridad. La quinta señal de que una persona es rica en secreto es que hace preguntas detalladas sobre compras de menos de cien dólares, pero no pestañea ante gastos grandes y necesarios. Esto es bastante común. Podrías encontrar a una persona rica pasando de veinte a treinta minutos investigando la mejor batería portátil de veinte dólares en Amazon. Leen reseñas, se clavan viendo videos de YouTube, pero de repente, los verás soltar quince mil dólares en un techo nuevo sin pestañear. La diferencia es que la compra de la batería portátil es un gusto, mientras que el techo era una necesidad. Probablemente hay quince opciones diferentes de baterías portátiles que hacen lo mismo, así que la persona rica se toma su tiempo para encontrar el mejor valor por su dinero. El reemplazo del techo de quince mil dólares, por otro lado, es una necesidad, y especialmente si el reemplazo es urgente, simplemente gastarán el dinero para acelerar esa reparación. Personalmente, creo que esto es más un hábito arraigado de alguien que se hizo rico por su propio esfuerzo. Este pequeño hábito de no gastar de más en compras pequeñas a lo largo del año puede sumar un gran ahorro en el transcurso de trescientos sesenta y cinco días. Sin embargo, la gente rica entiende que los grandes gastos necesarios ocurrirán, y cuando esas cosas surgen, tienen que actuar rápida y decisivamente. La señal número seis de que alguien es rico en secreto: ha dominado la espera. Mientras los demás compran cosas al instante, la gente rica investigará una compra por meses, esperará la oferta perfecta o, a veces, decidirá que no necesita ese artículo. También entienden que la mayoría de las ofertas no son reales. Y si esperas lo suficiente, casi todo se pone en liquidación. Si te pones a pensar en lo que es una oferta, es literalmente un descuento inventado basado en un número inventado. ¿Y quién establece ese número? Generalmente es el proveedor o el vendedor de dicho artículo. Una persona rica en secreto podría ser muy frugal y orientada al valor, no le importará esperar, especialmente si eso significa obtener algo que realmente ama, en lugar de algo con lo que solo está conforme. Entonces, una buena lección aquí es que si estás pensando en una compra grande, esperes al menos de siete a treinta días, realmente tienes que hacer una pausa y preguntarte si de verdad seguirás queriendo ese artículo, digamos, dentro de un mes. Y quizá podrías pensar en artículos sustitutos que cumplan el mismo propósito y que no te cuesten un dineral. Esto es solo gratificación postergada, el concepto clave para volverte muy, muy rico más adelante en la vida. A veces, debes renunciar a lo que quieres ahora por una vida financiera más sólida y un mejor futuro. La mayoría no puede postergar la gratificación ni veinticuatro horas, mucho menos cinco o diez años, una disciplina clave para la riqueza. Los ricos en secreto son muy pacientes, esperan no solo ofertas, sino también otras cosas importantes en la vida. La señal número siete: viven en una casa modesta, muy por debajo de su capacidad de pago. Mientras la mayoría se estira con hipotecas apenas pagables, los ricos en secreto hacen lo opuesto. Viven en casas que parecen casi demasiado pequeñas o modestas para alguien con su nivel de ingresos, o simplemente son dueños de su casa y nunca te darían a entender que viven sin hipoteca. Si te compras una casa grande y lujosa que apenas puedes pagar, no solo tendrás más metros cuadrados que decorar, sino que probablemente necesitarás limpiadores, jardineros, y si tienes alberca, un alberquero. Y una vez que tienes una casa grande, no querrás una más pequeña. Tendrás la expectativa de mantener las apariencias para vecinos, amigos y familiares, lo que implica gastar más dinero para mantener tu estilo de vida. Hay muchos niveles de riqueza. Si el objetivo es vivir tu vida más plena, serás más feliz, en mi opinión, si simplemente te mantienes callado. Compartí este tuit en un video anterior, pero creo que resume muy bien los diferentes niveles de riqueza. Brandon Turner (Twitter/X) dice que hay tres niveles de riqueza. Nivel uno: cortas tu césped porque no tienes dinero. Nivel dos: pagas a alguien porque tienes algo de dinero y valoras tu tiempo. Nivel tres: cortas tu césped porque, ya retirado, tienes tanta libertad y tiempo que quieres hacerlo tú mismo, por puro placer. Esto es muy acertado, pues la persona en el nivel tres es probablemente la más rica, disfrutando su vida y cortando su césped por el simple gusto de hacerlo. ¡Gracias por ver el video! Suscríbanse a Hump Days si no lo han hecho. Si les interesa mi próximo video sobre cómo llegar al uno por ciento superior, tengo consejos geniales... ahí. Querrán verlo aquí. Los veo en ese video, o en otro. Gracias por acompañarme. Paz.

When ego gets involved, you end up spending your time defending it and explaining it instead of pursuing it. So, I wanted to invest enough of my personal time in creating this crazy idea that I had. I worked at night and on the weekends on it until I knew I wouldn't turn back when I got doubt. You know, even if this is a good idea, the big guys will knock you off in 6 months and you've spent your life savings on it." Had I heard those things the moment that I had the idea, I would probably still be selling fax machines or something else cuz faxes aren't really around anymore. But it's very true because ideas are the most vulnerable in the moment you have them. And it's also people's instinct to share the idea in the most vulnerable stage because you get an idea and you turn to your co-worker, your husband, your wife, and you say, "Hey, I've got an idea." And out of love, they'll give you a list of like, "Hey, maybe you should consider this." And I didn't want that to happen. Based on the latest data and reports, there is strong evidence that housing affordability in 2025 is indeed at crisis levels, much higher than in previous decades. Reports highlight that home prices have risen dramatically—home prices relative to median household income are at an all-time high. The median price of a single-family home is now out of reach for roughly 75% of U.S. households. 

Renters also face significant burdens. For the lowest-income renters, there's a severe shortage: only about 35 affordable and available homes exist for every 100 extremely low-income renter households. In addition, rents have grown more quickly than wages over the past few years. Many renters are now paying more than half their income on rent.

Compared to 40 or 50 years ago, the affordability picture has worsened significantly. While rental costs may have seemed stable over longer horizons, the key factor is that home prices have far outpaced income growth, and rental costs have also climbed faster than wages in the last two decades. 

So, while some may argue that renting has remained relatively stable over half a century, the broader housing affordability crisis today—when factoring in homeownership and the overall cost of living—is more acute than in past generations.

property I didn't live in it I rented it out and it put money in my pocket before we get into how you can buy real estate with literally zero money let's differentiate between assets and liabilities if we need to summarize the topic assets are investments that put money in your pocket while liabilities take money out of your pocket your personal residence may be a liability due to ongoing expenses but rental properties can be assets that generate income to explain the situation even better let's take a look at an example imagine you have a personal residence that costs you $2,000 per month in mortgage payments property taxes insurance and maintenance this property is where you live and while it provides you with shelter it doesn't generate any income now let's consider a rental property suppose you invest in a rental property that you can acquire with a mortgage and the total monthly expenses including the mortgage payment property taxes Insurance maintenance and Property Management fees amount to $1,500 as a landlord you decide to rent out the property for $2,500 per month after deducting the $1,500 in expenses you're left with a positive cash flow of $11,000 per month in this scenario your personal residence is a liability as it costs you $2,000 per month which is money flowing out of your pocket on the other hand the rental property is an asset generating income of $1,000 per month which is flowing into your pocket now that we understand assets and liabilities it's time to learn deeply about debt before I can show you how to buy real estate with zero money when thinking about debt as a concept lots of negative and depressing thoughts come into mind but should they Robert kosaki introduced the concept of good debt and bad debt good debt is when you borrow money to acquire assets that generate cash flow and bad debt is the money you borrow to buy a new iPhone imagine this you come across a beautiful duplex property in a prime location the purchase price is $200,000 but you don't have all the funds available instead of dismissing the opportunity you decide to tap into the concept of good debt as introduced by the renowned Financial expert Robert kosaki you approach a lender and secure a mortgage for 80% of the property's value which amounts to $160,000 this is the borrowed money that will enable you to acquire the property and potentially generate cash flow with the duplex now in your possession you rent out both units each for $1,500 per month after accounting for expenses such as insurance and maintenance costs let's say your total monthly expenses come to $2500 here's where the magic of good debt comes into play the combined rental income from both units adds up to $3,000 per month after deducting the $2,500 in expenses you're left with a positive cash flow of $500 per month now let's break it down you're essentially using the borrowed money to acquire an asset the duplex property that is generating cash flow the rental income you receive covers the property's expenses and even leaves you with a positive Cash Flow It's a winwin situation good debt puts money in your pocket could a credit card be good debt well I just depends what you spend the money on right so if you spent it on something that's going to produce income for you like an asset then it's good debt but if you spend it on let's say a jacket that you wanted from Burberry then it's not first property there eight was in Maui Hawaii in the 1970s I bought it with a credit card wow property was $118,000 my first but I've taken several real estate classes and so I knew what to look for I found this one property it took a long time when I found it I just broke up my credit card and bought it and it put $25 in my pocket it went from this little dinky little thing this is years ago $25 it was good debt I wish I never sold it I don't you know it was a big mistake to sell it because today it's probably worth 4 or 500,000 and um that's a whole another story so far we've learned about assets liabil ities good debt and bad debt but in order to understand and to continue our journey on how to buy real estate with zero money there's another concept that we need to make ourselves familiar with maybe the most important one and it's called net operating income and capitalization rate net operating income or noi and capitalization rate or cap rate play Vital roles in real estate noi is the income generated from a property minus expenses which Banks evaluate to determine loan amounts the cap rate helps determine a property's value based on its noi net operating income and capitalization rate are fundamental concepts in the realm of real estate investment let's take a closer look at each of these factors and their significance net operating income represents the income generated from a property after deducting all operating expenses it's a key indicator of the property's profitability and cash flow potential to calculate the noi you subtract expenses such as property taxes Insurance maintenance costs management fees and vacancies from the property's total income it's particularly important when it comes to obtaining financing for Real Estate Investments Banks and financial institutions evaluate the noi to determine the loan amounts they're willing to provide a higher noi indicates a greater capacity to generate income and repay the loan increasing the likelihood of securing financing for your real estate Ventures capitalization rate is another crucial metric used in real estate valuation it's a percentage that represents the relationship between the property's net operating income and its overall value the cap rate helps investors assess the return on investment or Roi and determine the property's Worth to calculate the cap rate you divide the property's noi by its current market value the resulting percentage indicates the rate of return an investor can expect to earn on their investment based on the property's income generating potential for example if a property has an noi of $50,000 and is valued at $1 million the cap rate would be 5% $50,000 divided by $1 million this means that an investor can anticipate a 5% return on their investment annually the cap rate serves as a benchmark for comparing different investment opportunities and assessing their relative value a higher cap rate suggests a potentially higher return on investment while a lower cap rate may indicate a more stable and desirable property but with a lower return by understanding and analyzing the net operating income or noi and capitalization rate or cap rate of a property investors can make informed decisions about their real estate Investments these metrics provide valuable insights into a property's financial performance and help determine its attractiveness as an investment opportunity now here's the concept that you've all been waiting for after Mr kiyosaki's explanation we will reveal the six steps on how to buy a house with zero money it may sound like a Cheesy scheme but here it is making infinite money without using any money okay so what we're going to talk about today is an infinite return and uh very simply is if you have your real Financial education you'll never need money and you'll never pay taxes but they don't teach that in school and by the way we you know I've been working with the real estate guys for 20 years now and Tom and I will be on their Summit AT see and this year the theme is the infinite return so if you can there's only there only a few spaces left on the cruise but many of my advisers will be with me on that with Tom and I on this cruise and the whole theme is how you make money with nothing because you don't need money to make money it's one of the biggest lies and when people say that money doesn't make you happy well working at a job you hate doesn't make you happy and paying a lot of tax doesn't make me happy so to be able to do that we need to understand leveraging debt as complex as it sounds it's actually pretty simple leveraging debt in real estate refers to using borrowed funds such as a mortgage or a loan to acquire a property the idea behind leveraging debt is to use other people's money or OPM to finance the purchase rather than solely relying on your own funds by doing so you can amplify your potential returns and accelerate your wealth building process here are the six steps on how leveraging debt can lead to infinite returns one borrowing money when you secure a mortgage or loan to purchase a property you're leveraging debt this means you're using a portion of your own funds along with even better of bank's funds to finance the property make an appointment with your Bank advisor and ask him or her how you can increase your credit rating two proving the property after acquiring the property you have the opportunity to increase its value through improvements and Renovations this might include upgrading the kitchen adding extra rooms or enhancing the overall Aesthetics and functionality three increasing net operating income or noi as you improve the property you can potentially increase its rental income or occupancy rates by doing so you're boosting the property's net operating income noi is calculated by subtracting operating expenses such as maintenance costs property taxes and management fees from the property's income four refinancing once you've completed the property improvements and increase the noi you can approach the lender to refinance the property refinancing involves obtaining a new loan that pays off the original mortgage and it allows you to tap into the property's increased equity to understand this even better imagine a real estate investor who purchased a rental property a few years ago over time he made significant improvements to the property increasing its net operating income and boosting its overall value now he decides to take advantage of the increased Equity through refinancing he approaches his lender and presents the property's improved financial performance and the subsequent rise in its value after a thorough evaluation the lender agrees to refinance the property they off off him a new loan that pays off the original mortgage while providing him with access to the property's increased Equity five recouping initial investment the refinanced loan amount can be equal to or higher than the property's initial purchase price depending on the new appraised value if the refinanced loan covers or exceeds your initial investment including the down payment and renovation costs you essentially get your initial investment back six ongoing ownership and cash flow after refinancing you still retain ownership of the property and continue to generate rental income at this point the rental income surpasses the mortgage payments operating expenses and Debt Service resulting in positive Cash Flow by employing this strategy you've effectively recouped your initial investment while still owning the property the cash flow generated from rental income can now be considered infinite returns because you're no longer using your own money to generate profits real estate investing also offers significant tax benefits debt repayment is considered tax-free and you can deduct expenses and depreciation reducing taxable income it's a win-win situation for investors it's important to note that leveraging debt carries risks and it requires careful analysis market research and financial planning now let's debunk the myth that real estate is a slowlane approach to wealth while it's not a get-rich quick scheme real estate provides stability and consistent cash flow it offers long-term wealth building potential that can transform your financial future you should always consider real estate as a viable investment option but not the only one check out our video where we cover the four assets that Robert kosaki thinks are safer than cash

For many Californians, owning a home has never been more expensive, more complicated, or more uncertain. After years of record prices and punishing interest rates, the so-called recovery is looking fragile. Home prices remain among the highest in the nation. But affordability keeps slipping. Insurance premiums, especially for wildfire and coastal coverage, have soared nearly 20% since last year. And despite mild cooling in some metros, the population exodus hasn't stopped. More than 300,000 people left the Golden State in 2025, chasing lower taxes, cheaper housing, and a more sustainable life elsewhere. The problem, too few buyers, too much inventory, and rising risks that most families can't afford to ignore. From the wildfire belt of Southern California to the overbuilt suburbs of the Bay Area, warning signs are flashing across the map. Realtors may still sell the sunshine, but the math no longer works. In some cities, home ownership has become a financial trap, one that could quietly drain savings, wealth, and stability for years to come. The city of angel's luxury and relentless ambition is entering 2026 under heavy pressure. Behind the movie lights and palm lined streets, the housing reality is far from glamorous. For a city built on dreams, home ownership has become its biggest illusion. Number nine, Oakland. Once the beating heart of Bay Area culture and diversity is now facing a hard reckoning in 2026. Since 2024, more than 11,000 people have left the city, taking their tax dollars, their businesses, and their optimism with them. Meanwhile, wildfire exposure and aging infrastructure have driven insurance costs up by nearly 40% in hillside neighborhoods. Yet, through all this, Oakland still has soul. The murals, the music, the resilience, but soul alone can't sell a house. The market is tired, the buyers are wary, and the foundation feels shaky. Number eight, Stockton. A city that once symbolized resilience after the Great Recession is now watching that hard one progress start to slip away. In 2026, the cracks are widening again. That gap might not look shocking by California standards, but for Stockton, it's the kind of imbalance that sparks a slow unraveling. Families who stretched to buy during the pandemic boom are now dealing with adjustable rate resets, inflated insurance, and shrinking equity. Foreclosures are up 15% year-over-year, and nearly one in four listings slash their asking price in the first quarter of 2026. On average, homes sit unsold for 50 or more days, a stark contrast to the bidding wars and cash offers of just a few years ago. Meanwhile, crime remains a persistent scar. Stockton's violent crime rate sits among the state's highest, eroding buyer confidence and pushing investors to safer ground. And even as new construction continues to sprawl on the city's outskirts, demand isn't keeping up. Empty houses are rising faster than occupied ones. What's happening in Stockton isn't dramatic. It's gradual. And that's what makes it dangerous. A slow squeeze on working families, a fading sense of security, and a market that feels increasingly hollow. Number seven, San Diego. A city of endless summer ocean breezes and postcard perfection. It's the kind of place that makes you believe life here must be worth any price. But in 2026, that sunshine is masking a storm. That imbalance forces many homeowners to spend more than 45% of their income on housing alone before even counting insurance or taxes. and insurance has become San Diego's silent crisis. With wildfire exposure on the eastern edges, coastal erosion along the shoreline, and rising sea levels creeping closer each year, premiums have jumped nearly 60% since 2020. Some insurers have pulled out completely, leaving homeowners scrambling for limited high-cost coverage. Even the once booming condo market downtown is cooling. Developers overbuilt demand has softened and more than one in four listings in early 2026 saw price cuts. Property taxes among the state's highest and new water usage fees have further tightened household budgets. San Diego still looks like Paradise Palm Trees perfect weather a thriving food scene. But behind that postcard view lies one of the most financially strained housing markets in the country. The city's lifestyle premium has become a liability. A beautiful trap for those who buy at the wrong time. Number six, Burbank. That makes it one of the least affordable housing markets in the entire United States, where even middle inome families spend more than half their gross earnings just to keep up with mortgage payments. Santa Cruz still has that irresistible magic, the boardwalk, the surf, the sunsets that turn the horizon gold. But for most buyers, the cost of living that dream has become financially reckless. Number four, Santa Barbara, where Spanish architecture meets California's Mediterranean coast, a city long hailed as paradise for the elite. But in 2026, this peaceful isolation has become its greatest liability. The average home price now sits above $655,000 reaches $62,000. That imbalance has priced out most local workers and forced many families to relocate inland. What's left behind is a shrinking aging population and a housing market that's slowly freezing in place. Insurance costs are climbing fast with wildfire exposure across forested zones and storm damage along the coast. Homeowners here pay an average of $4,900 per year for basic coverage. Some carriers have stopped writing new policies altogether, leaving buyers to rely on expensive statebacked plans. Infrastructure is another silent crisis. Roads buckle during winter rains power outages are common, and broadband remains unreliable in many parts of the county. Access to health care and essential services is limited, which makes relocation, not residency, the growing trend. In the first quarter of 20 to 26, 35% of listings saw price reductions the highest in Northern California. Yes, Mendescino is still breathtaking. The kind of place where fog rolls in like poetry and time seems to slow. But beauty doesn't pay the bills and tranquility can't sustain a tax base. Mendescino isn't collapsing overnight. It's eroding one family, one insurer, one empty house at a time. Number one, San Francisco, the city of innovation icons and impossible views once define the future of urban living. But in 2026, that future looks far less certain. The Golden Gate still glows at sunset. Yet beneath it, the city's housing foundation is cracking. The median home price hovers around $1.3 million, among the highest in the nation. Meanwhile, the median household income stands near 125,000. Impressive on paper, but not nearly enough to offset housing insurance and tax burdens that continue to soar. The pandemic exodus has left a lasting mark. Since 2021, more than 60,000 residents have left San Francisco, including thousands of tech professionals and families who once powered its economy. Downtown vacancy rates remain above 30% and nearly 1 in three homes listed in early 2026 saw a price cut. Crime and cost of living fears have compounded the damage. Property theft and vandalism persist in commercial corridors, discouraging both small business owners and wouldbe buyers. Insurance premiums now top $6,000 a year, while HOA fees for condos routinely exceed 2,000 per month. Even luxury homes are sitting on the market longer than ever. A sign that prestige alone no longer guarantees profit. San Francisco's identity hasn't disappeared. The creativity, the energy, the unmatched scenery, but it's been hollowed out by cost and consequence. The city still carries weight, but not the same hope. California's housing story in 2026 isn't about sudden collapse. It's about a slow, grinding correction. Prices haven't crashed, but the illusion of endless growth has. Across the Golden State, the same pattern repeats sky-high costs, shrinking affordability, and a mass migration, reshaping the map, one moving truck at a time. First, interest rates remain high enough to choke affordability even as prices plateau. Second, insurance and property taxes are climbing faster than incomes, especially in coastal and wildfirrone zones. And third, inventory is quietly stacking up more sellers, fewer qualified buyers, and a growing fear that the peak has already passed. But not everything is bleak for patient buyers and smart investors. A market like this offers opportunity. As overvalued cities correct new regions from the Inland Empire to parts of northern Nevada are starting to look more promising. The key isn't to panic, it's to prepare. So whether you're planning to buy, sell, or simply survive this housing storm, remember this real estate isn't just about location anymore, it's about timing. And timing right now favors caution over emotion. Which city shocked you the most in this list? Let us know in the comments and tell us if you've seen similar trends in your area. He focuses on the financial reality, but the financial analysis and the political speech are connected. Trump is using the rally to fundraise off the judgment. They're trying to take my money. Help me fight back while simultaneously managing the political fallout. The nasty speech is the product pitch. Cohen's financial analysis is the reality check. What Cohen is describing in liquidation of assets is the specific consequence that Trump's narrative of persecution is designed to prevent by winning the election. If Trump wins the presidency, the specific legal dynamics change. The federal cases can be ended through DOJ direction. The bond requirements might be addressed through various mechanisms and the political pressure on the New York cases may increase. Trump's political strategy and his legal strategy are inseparable. The rally in Michigan is not separate from the legal proceedings in New York. It's the fundraising apparatus, the political narrative, and the legal escape hatch all operating simultaneously. Here's the specific argument Cohen is making about Trump's finances that deserves more development. When he says Trump is probably looking at RNC money and super PAC money, he's identifying a pattern that predates this specific judgment. Throughout Trump's first term and second candidacy period, various Trump-affiliated entities have paid legal bills using donor funds. The Save America PAC, which Trump controls, paid tens of millions in legal fees for Trump and his associates. The argument for why this is permissible, Trump's legal battles are related to his political activities and donors who understand this are funding the fight willingly. But a 464 million dollar judgment in a civil fraud case is not a campaign-related legal expense in any normal understanding of that term. The Trump organization's fraud happened years before Trump was a political candidate. The civil fraud case is about the Trump organization's business practices from 2011 to 2021. Using donor money to pay a judgment for business fraud as opposed to legal fees for campaign-related proceedings is legally and ethically different. This is the distinction Cohen is gesturing at when he says Trump is looking at these funding sources. He doesn't mean Trump will definitely use them. He means Trump's first instinct is always to find money from somewhere other than his own pocket. Atlantic City casino bankruptcies left the bondholders paying. The Trump shuttle failure left the banks paying. The contractor payments that were never made left the contractors bearing the losses. The pattern is consistent. Trump finds ways to have other people absorb his financial obligations. The question in 2024 is whether that pattern can work when the obligation is not a business failure but a civil fraud judgment entered by a court. Courts have enforcement mechanisms. James had the authority to register the judgment in multiple counties and execute against specific properties. The shitty markets refusal to write a 464 million dollar bond is not the same as a bank deciding to walk away from a bad loan. It's a reflection of what Trump's actual assets can support as collateral. Cohen's analysis, for all its insider speculation, is ultimately validated by the bond crisis. 30 companies said no. The appellate division had to reduce the requirement to 175 million dollars. Don Hankey's Knight Specialty Insurance posted the bond using cash collateral. All of this confirmed what Cohen was saying in this morning interview. The claimed cash position and the actual ability to collateralize half a billion dollars are not the same thing. One final thought on what Cohen represents in this specific moment. He's the person who knows Trump the best or one of them who is speaking publicly and critically. Michael Cohen spent 11 years as Trump's fixer doing the things Trump wanted done without asking too many questions. He committed crimes on Trump's behalf. He went to prison. And now he's the most prominent insider voice warning about what Trump is capable of and what Trump's actual situation is. The complicated thing about Cohen as a source is that his credibility problems are real and his anger at Trump is genuine and documented. When he says Trump doesn't have the cash and will need to look at RNC money, he could be correct and motivated or he could be exaggerating Trump's financial difficulties out of antipathy. The bond crisis, 30 shy companies, the appellate division reduction is the check on Cohen speculation. And in this case, the bond crisis confirms the core of what Cohen is saying. Trump couldn't meet the original bond requirement. He needed a unusual arrangement and the overall financial obligation is in the range of what Cohen described. Cohen predicted liquidation. That specific outcome, actual forced asset sales, didn't materialize because the bond was reduced. The bond was posted and the Engeron penalty was later vacated on Eighth Amendment grounds. But the financial stress Cohen described was real. The bond crisis was real. The inability to simply write a check for 464 million dollars was real. Cohen's insider knowledge, even discounted for his complicated relationship with the truth and with Trump, pointed in the right direction. And the rally speech, coordinated and overly nasty, is Trump's answer to all of it. Not engagement with the fraud finding, not acknowledgement of what the documents showed, just they're nasty, they're coordinated, and they want to stop me. For his base in Michigan that February weekend, that message was sufficient. For Engeron, who had watched Trump and his witnesses deny reality for months in his courtroom, it was the confirmation of everything his final judgment would say about the defendant's character. Here's the broader significance of this specific moment. February 2024, Cohen on CNN, Trump at a rally in Michigan. This is the period when the legal and political stories about Trump are most directly colliding. The Engeron judgment came on February 16th. The Carroll verdict had come on January 26th. - BG Wealth Sharing Review - Legit or Scam Platform?: Kind: captions Language: en BG Wealth Sharing sounds impressive, right? They market themselves as this sophisticated financial platform, even calling it the world's largest hedge fund. They say they use advanced trading strategies, blockchain, and digital currency systems to make money for investors. But here's the real question. Is BG Wealth Sharing truly legit or just another Ponzi scheme? Stick with me till the end and you'll see the truth. The first concern is transparency, or actually the lack of it. The company doesn't share who really runs it. The only name on their website is Professor Steven Beard, and after digging, it turns out he doesn't even exist outside their site. That's a huge red flag. The second concern is their fake history. They claim they were founded in 2018, but in reality, they've been around for less than a year. Their websites are privately registered and constantly changing and they funnel people into DSJ exchange. Another fake trading platform already flagged for fraud. The third concern is the corporate setup. They say their SEC registered under BG wealth sharing Ltd, but that's just a Colorado shell company created in April 2025 by a fake accounting firm that doesn't even exist. and the address they use. It's just a rented mailbox at Postnet, not a real office. The fourth concern is regulator warnings. Authorities already know what's going on. In May 2025, the UK's Financial Conduct Authority, the FCA, issued a securities fraud warning against both BG Wealth Sharing and DSJ Exchange. So, regulators themselves are calling this a scam. The fifth concern is the business model. There are no real products, no real services. All they do is get people to invest USDT, promise up to 2.6% daily returns, and push members to recruit others. That's the textbook definition of a Ponzi scheme. The sixth concern is their so-called SEC compliance. They make it look like they're SEC registered, but all they really did was file as an exempt reporting adviser. That doesn't make them legal. And under the Howie test, their setup clearly qualifies as an unregistered securities offering, which is illegal in most countries. So, is BG Wealth Sharing legit? No, not at all. It's just another Ponzi scheme hiding behind fake executives, a false history, and a shell company to look official. They promise impossible returns. They rely completely on recruitment, and regulators have already flagged them. In the end, this scheme will collapse, and when it does, most investors will lose their money. - 10 Surprising Reasons Nobody Moves to Luxembourg: Kind: captions Language: en Why do people purposefully&nbsp; steer clear of Luxembourg? &nbsp; The country ranks pretty high when&nbsp; talking about the standards of living.&nbsp;&nbsp; The quality of life is great, and a lot&nbsp; of the locals live there comfortably. But, surprisingly, there are several&nbsp; reasons why many people simply don’t&nbsp;&nbsp; move to Luxembourg, and today, we’ll&nbsp; look at ten reasons why this happens. 10. Expensive Housing You can find several houses that are up for grabs,&nbsp;&nbsp; so there’s no shortage of places to live.&nbsp; Most of them can be found in great locations,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the standard of living is pretty high. But as soon as you look at the prices,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can almost feel your wallet emptying.&nbsp; Residence in Luxembourg is extremely expensive,&nbsp;&nbsp; and not everyone is rolling in enough&nbsp; dough to snag a pricey property. Even renting a cozy studio in the suburbs will&nbsp; set you back 1,400 euros every month. Setting&nbsp;&nbsp; this amount aside is very hard, especially if&nbsp; you’re young and just beginning your career&nbsp;&nbsp; in the country. Plus, if you’ve moved with your&nbsp; family and are looking for a bigger place to live,&nbsp;&nbsp; expenses will get very tough to manage. Most people who want to move to the region&nbsp;&nbsp; would rather move to Germany, where housing&nbsp; is a lot cheaper compared to Luxembourg. So, no matter how great it&nbsp; must be to live in Luxembourg,&nbsp;&nbsp; there’s no point thinking about moving if&nbsp; you can’t afford it in the first place. 9. Cost of Living Just like housing,&nbsp;&nbsp; everything else can get super expensive as well.&nbsp; Everyday items like food and groceries will&nbsp;&nbsp; drain your funds quicker than you can expect. And it’s not just a minor difference in costs;&nbsp;&nbsp; most people who live there try not&nbsp; to get their items locally. Instead,&nbsp;&nbsp; they dash across borders to snag deals. This&nbsp; may be one of the very few places in the world&nbsp;&nbsp; where shopping from abroad saves them money. Like everywhere else in the world, inflation&nbsp;&nbsp; has also been rising in Luxembourg, which has&nbsp; only made costs worse. This is mostly fueled by&nbsp;&nbsp; index hikes. As costs increase for companies and&nbsp; business owners, they pass this on to the buyer,&nbsp;&nbsp; who in turn has to pay more for products. Several other countries around the world&nbsp;&nbsp; offer lower costs of living, and if someone is&nbsp; planning to move on a tight budget, Luxembourg&nbsp;&nbsp; is a place they steer clear of. 8. It’s Hard to Find Jobs Now, don’t get me wrong; there are several&nbsp; job opportunities that you can avail,&nbsp;&nbsp; but the job market isn’t the same&nbsp; as it used to be a few years ago. The pay is pretty satisfactory, with the legal&nbsp; minimum wage cruising comfortably above €2,000&nbsp;&nbsp; each month. This might even make managing expenses&nbsp; easy for a lot of people, Luxembourg's not just&nbsp;&nbsp; a tiny country; it's a tourist hotspot.&nbsp; This newfound fame comes with a twist,&nbsp;&nbsp; as jobs are getting picky, demanding language&nbsp; fluency and a large number of qualifications. Several people might not have the high level&nbsp; of experience that Luxembourg jobs demand. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; it isn’t about the lack of jobs –&nbsp; it’s about the lack of suitable jobs,&nbsp;&nbsp; where an average person faces severe difficulty&nbsp; finding a job that matches their qualification. Now, if you're thinking of diving into unskilled&nbsp; gigs, especially in the field of hospitality,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s going to be a tough ride for you. The&nbsp; paycheck barely covers the basics. You could&nbsp;&nbsp; afford rent and groceries, but you’ll be left&nbsp; with nothing to save or to spend on leisure. Plus, speaking only English won't always cut&nbsp; it. In a country where French and German are&nbsp;&nbsp; prevalent, scoring a job without mastering one&nbsp; of these languages is almost impossible. Being&nbsp;&nbsp; monolingual will get you nothing in Luxembourg,&nbsp; so people who aren’t especially proficient in&nbsp;&nbsp; languages mostly stay away from this country. 7. The Disparity between Locals and Foreigners Most of the locals in Luxembourg are very&nbsp; detached from the non-locals in the region.&nbsp;&nbsp; They live in the posh public sector, where the&nbsp; cash flows, and they usually only interact with&nbsp;&nbsp; people in their circle of earnings and wealth. And it’s not just a small part of the population;&nbsp;&nbsp; almost 90,000 out of 115,000 Luxembourgers&nbsp; have bagged those civil servant badges,&nbsp;&nbsp; which lets them maintain a comfortable standard&nbsp; of living. For many Luxembourgers, it's all about&nbsp;&nbsp; those stable admin or public sector jobs. So, it’s no surprise that most Luxembourg&nbsp;&nbsp; locals have their own ways of communicating with&nbsp; each other. It’s like they’ve all got this secret&nbsp;&nbsp; language, and if you’re a foreigner, you end up&nbsp; in your little corner, hanging out with fellow&nbsp;&nbsp; outsiders. The local language is almost like&nbsp; trying to solve a puzzle, and they don’t try to&nbsp;&nbsp; make it easy for you to get involved with them. Instead of integrating into society,&nbsp;&nbsp; whoever moved there feels like they’re living&nbsp; in parallel universes—Luxembourgers in one,&nbsp;&nbsp; foreigners in another. The only ray of hope&nbsp; people might see is that 45% of the people&nbsp;&nbsp; in Luxembourg are expats. So, you may not&nbsp; need to interact with the locals at all. Still, you’ll get to learn nothing about the&nbsp; culture there if you don’t mingle with the locals,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is why you’d never feel at home. 6. The Visa System is Tricky Now, even if you decide to move to Luxembourg&nbsp; and you’re ready with your bags packed,&nbsp;&nbsp; it won’t be that easy. At this point, things&nbsp; will get super complicated with the visa&nbsp;&nbsp; business that you’ll have to handle. Luxembourg, being a part of the EU,&nbsp;&nbsp; has its visa game on a tiered system.&nbsp; If you're an EU citizen, lucky you;&nbsp;&nbsp; you won’t have to face any visa drama, but you&nbsp; will still have to get a registration certificate&nbsp;&nbsp; after three months of living in Luxembourg. Now, if you're from the UK, you can chill&nbsp;&nbsp; in Luxembourg for three months&nbsp; visa-free, but if you want to work,&nbsp;&nbsp; it won’t be that simple. Before entering the&nbsp; country, you will have to get a temporary&nbsp;&nbsp; pass from the Ministry of Foreign and European&nbsp; Affairs. Plus, you’ll need a job offer in your&nbsp;&nbsp; back pocket before starting this visa journey. After that, you can eventually get your hands&nbsp;&nbsp; on a residence permit. But in case&nbsp; you aren’t from the EU or London,&nbsp;&nbsp; the system isn’t designed to make things easy&nbsp; for you. Getting a work visa, in particular,&nbsp;&nbsp; will be almost impossible. In short, your employer&nbsp; would need to interact with the authorities and&nbsp;&nbsp; proclaim that they couldn’t find anyone else&nbsp; with your qualifications in all of the EU. You simply have to charm the official&nbsp; big shots for a work visa, and if you&nbsp;&nbsp; want to stay and keep working, you need to&nbsp; earn over 2 thousand dollars a month. And&nbsp;&nbsp; don't even get me started on the paperwork;&nbsp; you have to fill up so many forms that most&nbsp;&nbsp; people would just give up trying to move there. Sounds messed up, but that’s exactly why most&nbsp;&nbsp; people wouldn’t move to the country. 5. Citizenship is Tough To cut things short, there’s no ticket to&nbsp; Luxembourg citizenship through cash. If&nbsp;&nbsp; you move there, and you like things enough&nbsp; to wanna stay there forever, they certainly&nbsp;&nbsp; won’t make that decision easy for you. There are several options for you to get&nbsp;&nbsp; your citizenship, one of which is through&nbsp; family ties. If you have Luxembourgish kin,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s the easiest way to get your citizenship. The&nbsp; second option is citizenship by naturalization,&nbsp;&nbsp; which means that you live there for a long while&nbsp; and prove that you’re valuable to the country. The issue resides in the fact that you have to&nbsp; spend at least seven years living in Luxembourg&nbsp;&nbsp; to be eligible for citizenship. You can’t vanish&nbsp; in between, and once you’ve completed your period,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can hit up the Ministry of Foreign Affairs&nbsp; to start your long-term resident status. However, there are several conditions attached&nbsp; to this process. For starters, you need to be&nbsp;&nbsp; at least 18 years old, which is fine. But&nbsp; then you also have to pass a speaking test,&nbsp;&nbsp; where you should be fully fluent in&nbsp; the Luxembourg dialect and language. It will also be mandatory for you to&nbsp; take three civil instruction classes&nbsp;&nbsp; so that you’re well aware of Luxembourg’s&nbsp; culture and also meet their expectations of&nbsp;&nbsp; a squeaky-clean residency record. If any of these things are missing,&nbsp;&nbsp; you simply can’t apply for citizenship, which&nbsp; is scary enough to make people stay away from&nbsp;&nbsp; Luxembourg altogether. 4. It Can Be Boring This is how Luxembourg is perceived by the outside&nbsp; world; you will find several Reddit threads&nbsp;&nbsp; talking about how boring the country is. But why? Well, you will feel the same way if you’re a&nbsp;&nbsp; fan of the nightlife. If you enjoy&nbsp; going out, drinking, and partying,&nbsp;&nbsp; Luxembourg doesn’t get into any of that stuff. They go to bed early and wake up early,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is why there are barely any bars&nbsp; or nightclubs in the entire country. 3. Lack of Good Educational Institutes Now, don’t get me wrong; the standard of&nbsp;&nbsp; education is super high in the country,&nbsp; just like in the rest of Western Europe.&nbsp;&nbsp; The issue lies in the fact that there are only&nbsp; two universities in the country, one of which&nbsp;&nbsp; is state-owned and the other is private. It can be almost impossible for a foreign&nbsp;&nbsp; student to get enrolled in one of these&nbsp; universities, as they are highly competitive and&nbsp;&nbsp; preference is given to local students. It’s also&nbsp; extremely difficult to acquire a student visa,&nbsp;&nbsp; so you won’t see a lot of young people&nbsp; moving to Luxembourg for higher studies. 2. Bad Climate Most people plan to relocate when they are tired&nbsp;&nbsp; of harsh, cold environments. A warm country offers&nbsp; comfort and is a huge attraction for foreigners,&nbsp;&nbsp; but that’s where Luxembourg loses the battle. You will rarely find the sun shining in this&nbsp;&nbsp; country, and most of the time, it’s cloudy&nbsp; and rainy. If you like the prospect of always&nbsp;&nbsp; having to walk around with an emergency&nbsp; umbrella and a raincoat, you might like&nbsp;&nbsp; this place. Otherwise, you won’t. 1. Kids Definitely Won’t Like it If you’ve got a family, you’d know that&nbsp; relocation has to offer advantages for&nbsp;&nbsp; every member of the family. Entertainment is&nbsp; crucial for kids to settle into a new place,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Luxembourg lacks in that sector severely. There are no amusement parks or theme parks in the&nbsp;&nbsp; country, which means that on a weekend, your kids&nbsp; simply won’t know what to do. Then there’s the&nbsp;&nbsp; lack of coastline and access to the sea. Living&nbsp; in a landlocked country makes entertainment even&nbsp;&nbsp; harder to come by, and most people wouldn’t wanna&nbsp; move there because there is nothing to do for fun.
